content:
# Exodus Chapter 6 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~34r–34v (PDF pp. 66–67); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 46–47 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_006_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_006_page_002.jpg`

---

**6:1** And YHWH said to Moses, "Now you shall see what I will do to Pharaoh, for by a strong hand he will send them out, and by a strong hand he will drive them out of his land."

**6:2** And God spoke to Moses and said to him, "I am YHWH.

**6:3** And I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob as El Shaddai, but by My name YHWH I was not known to them. [fn1]

**6:4** And I also established My covenant with them, to give them the land of Canaan, the land of their sojourning, in which they sojourned.

**6:5** And I also have heard the groaning of the sons of Israel, whom the Egyptians are enslaving, and I have remembered My covenant.

**6:6** Therefore say to the sons of Israel: 'I am YHWH, and I will bring you out from under the burdens of Egypt, and I will deliver you from their bondage, and I will redeem you with an outstretched arm and with great judgments. [fn2]

**6:7** And I will take you to Myself for a people, and I will be to you for God; and you shall know that I am YHWH your God, who brings you out from under the burdens of Egypt.

**6:8** And I will bring you into the land which I lifted up My hand to give to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob; and I will give it to you for a possession. I am YHWH.'" [fn3]

**6:9** And Moses spoke thus to the sons of Israel, but they did not listen to Moses, from shortness of spirit and from hard bondage. [fn4]

**6:10** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying:

**6:11** "Go, speak to Pharaoh king of Egypt, that he let the sons of Israel go from his land."

**6:12** And Moses spoke before YHWH, saying, "Behold, the sons of Israel have not listened to me; how then shall Pharaoh listen to me? And I am of uncircumcised lips." [fn5]

**6:13** And YHWH spoke to Moses and to Aaron, and He gave them a charge concerning the sons of Israel and concerning Pharaoh king of Egypt, to bring the sons of Israel out of the land of Egypt.

**6:14** These are the heads of their fathers' houses: The sons of Reuben, the firstborn of Israel -- Hanoch and Pallu, Hezron and Carmi. These are the clans of Reuben.

**6:15** And the sons of Simeon -- Jemuel and Jamin and Ohad and Jachin and Zohar and Shaul the son of a Canaanite woman. These are the clans of Simeon.

**6:16** And these are the names of the sons of Levi according to their generations: Gershon and Kohath and Merari. And the years of the life of Levi were one hundred and thirty-seven years. [fn6]

**6:17** The sons of Gershon -- Libni and Shimei, according to their clans.

**6:18** And the sons of Kohath -- Amram and Izhar and Hebron and Uzziel. And the years of the life of Kohath were one hundred and thirty-three years.

**6:19** And the sons of Merari -- Mahli and Mushi. These are the clans of the Levites according to their generations.

**6:20** And Amram took Jochebed his father's sister as his wife, and she bore to him Aaron and Moses. And the years of the life of Amram were one hundred and thirty-seven years. [fn7]

**6:21** And the sons of Izhar -- Korah and Nepheg and Zichri.

**6:22** And the sons of Uzziel -- Mishael and Elzaphan and Sithri.

**6:23** And Aaron took Elisheba, daughter of Amminadab, sister of Nahshon, as his wife; and she bore to him Nadab and Abihu, Eleazar and Ithamar.

**6:24** And the sons of Korah -- Assir and Elkanah and Abiasaph. These are the clans of the Korahites.

**6:25** And Eleazar son of Aaron took one of the daughters of Putiel as his wife; and she bore to him Phinehas. These are the heads of the fathers of the Levites according to their clans.

**6:26** This is the Aaron and Moses to whom YHWH said, "Bring out the sons of Israel from the land of Egypt according to their hosts." [fn8]

**6:27** They are the ones who spoke to Pharaoh king of Egypt, to bring out the sons of Israel from Egypt. This is Moses and Aaron.

**6:28** And it was on the day YHWH spoke to Moses in the land of Egypt,

**6:29** that YHWH spoke to Moses, saying, "I am YHWH. Speak to Pharaoh king of Egypt all that I am speaking to you."

**6:30** And Moses said before YHWH, "Behold, I am of uncircumcised lips, and how shall Pharaoh listen to me?" [fn5]

---

> **[fn1] v.3 -- El Shaddai and the divine name** -- Hebrew: *va'era el-Avraham el-Yitschaq ve'el-Ya'aqov be'El Shaddai u-shemi YHWH lo noda'ti lahem* (וָאֵרָא אֶל־אַבְרָהָם אֶל־יִצְחָק וְאֶל־יַעֲקֹב בְּאֵל שַׁדָּי וּשְׁמִי יְהוָה לֹא נוֹדַעְתִּי לָהֶם). This is among the most theologically significant verses in the Torah. All three translators retain "El Shaddai" untranslated, which the consensus follows -- it functions as a divine title whose etymology remains debated ("God Almighty," "God of the Mountain," "The Sufficient One"), and no single English word captures it. The critical clause is *lo noda'ti lahem*, "I was not known to them." The niphal of *yada'* means "I was not made known" -- passive, not merely negative. This does not mean the patriarchs never heard the tetragrammaton (Genesis uses YHWH freely from 2:4); it means the *experiential content* of the name -- YHWH as the God who acts to redeem, who breaks empires and keeps covenants by force -- was not yet revealed to them. The patriarchs knew God as the one who *promises*; this generation will know God as the one who *performs*. The LXX (Vaticanus) reads *ouk edelosa autois* ("I did not reveal to them"), an active verb, losing the niphal's nuance but preserving the theological claim. LOGOS and EMET both render "I was not known to them," following the MT's niphal. PNEUMA likewise renders "I was not known to them." The consensus follows the MT: "I was not known to them." The phrase "but by My name YHWH" uses "but" (following LOGOS and PNEUMA) to make explicit the adversative force of the Hebrew *waw* in *u-shemi*, which sets up the contrast between the patriarchal and Mosaic revelations of God.

> **[fn2] vv.6-8 -- The four "I will" promises** -- The theological backbone of the chapter. Four consecutive *weqatal* verbs form a staircase of divine commitment: (1) *vehotse'ti* -- "I will bring out" (hiphil of *yatsa'*: physical extraction from bondage); (2) *vehitsalti* -- "I will deliver" (hiphil of *natsal*: rescue from the power of the oppressor); (3) *vega'alti* -- "I will redeem" (*ga'al*: the kinsman-redeemer verb, the language of a family member buying back a relative from debt-slavery -- profoundly intimate); (4) *velaqakhti* -- "I will take" (the language of covenant union -- "I will take you as My people"). The movement escalates from external liberation to covenantal intimacy. A fifth verb in v.8, *veheve'ti* ("I will bring in"), extends the promise to the land. In later Jewish tradition these four (or five) promises correspond to the cups of wine at the Passover Seder. EMET renders all four with distinct verbs ("bring out... deliver... redeem... take"); LOGOS likewise ("bring out... deliver... redeem... take"); PNEUMA varies the English more freely ("bring out... rescue... redeem... take"). The consensus follows EMET and LOGOS: "bring out," "deliver," "redeem," "take" -- each semantically distinct, each preserving the Hebrew's escalation. For *sivlot* ("burdens"), the consensus retains "burdens" (EMET, LOGOS) rather than PNEUMA's "crushing weight." While PNEUMA's rendering conveys the suffering vividly, *sivlot* is a technical term for forced-labor corvee recurring from 1:11 and 5:4-5, and consistency requires a stable rendering. The term "bondage" for *avodah* (v.6) and the phrase "outstretched arm" (*zeroa' netuyah*) and "great judgments" (*shefatim gedolim*) are agreed upon by all three translators.

> **[fn3] v.8 -- "I lifted up My hand"** -- Hebrew *nasa'ti et-yadi*: the gesture of oath-taking. All three translators agree on the somatic imagery. EMET: "lifted up my hand"; LOGOS: "lifted up My hand"; PNEUMA: "swore with uplifted hand." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS, retaining the literal form: "I lifted up My hand." The idiom is functionally equivalent to "I swore," but the physical gesture carries covenantal weight (cf. Ezekiel 20:5-6, where the same expression describes the same event). PNEUMA's rendering explicitly glosses the gesture as an oath, which is interpretively correct but eliminates the idiom itself. For *morashah* ("possession/inheritance"), the consensus renders "possession" (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA agree), reflecting the permanent hereditary claim from the root *yarash* ("to possess/inherit").

> **[fn4] v.9 -- "Shortness of spirit"** -- Hebrew *mi-qotser ruach u-me'avodah qashah*. The noun *qotser* ("shortness, curtailment") from *qatsar* ("to be short, to cut short") combines with *ruach* ("spirit, breath") to create a psychosomatic image: breath too short to hope, spirit truncated by suffering. EMET: "*qotser ruach* [shortness of spirit]"; LOGOS: "shortness of spirit"; PNEUMA: "their spirits were crushed and their slavery too cruel." The consensus follows EMET and LOGOS: "from shortness of spirit and from hard bondage." The literal rendering preserves both the physical (*breath*) and psychological (*spirit*) dimensions of *ruach* without collapsing one into the other. PNEUMA's "spirits were crushed" captures the emotional reality beautifully but replaces the specific Hebrew image -- shortness, abbreviation, truncation -- with a more general metaphor of crushing. The LXX renders *oligopsychia* ("smallness of soul"), an excellent calque that confirms the psychic reading. The word "bondage" for *avodah qashah* follows LOGOS; EMET's "hard labor" and PNEUMA's "slavery too cruel" are both defensible, but "hard bondage" maintains the SOPHIA convention from 1:14 where *avodah qashah* first appears.

> **[fn5] vv.12, 30 -- "Uncircumcised of lips"** -- Hebrew *aral sefatayim* (עֲרַל שְׂפָתָיִם). The metaphor applies circumcision language to speech: as uncircumcised flesh is "blocked" or "sealed," so Moses' lips are obstructed. The same root *'arel* describes "uncircumcised hearts" (Leviticus 26:41) and "uncircumcised ears" (Jeremiah 6:10) -- organs closed to God's word. This is more extreme than the "heavy of mouth and heavy of tongue" of 4:10; here Moses frames his inadequacy in the vocabulary of sacred exclusion. EMET: "uncircumcised of lips"; LOGOS: "uncircumcised lips"; PNEUMA: "I am no one's idea of a speaker." The consensus follows EMET and LOGOS: "of uncircumcised lips." The literal rendering is non-negotiable here. The circumcision metaphor is theologically loaded -- it connects speech fitness to covenant fitness -- and any paraphrase severs that connection. PNEUMA's "no one's idea of a speaker" is rhetorically brilliant and captures the self-deprecating tone, but it loses the covenantal register entirely. The LXX renders *alogos* ("without speech") in v.12 and *ischnophonos* ("weak-voiced") in v.30, showing that even the ancient translators struggled with the metaphor; neither Greek rendering preserves the circumcision imagery. The SOPHIA consensus preserves it in both occurrences, maintaining the inclusio that frames the genealogy.

> **[fn6] vv.14-25 -- The genealogy: naming conventions** -- The genealogy uses standard English forms for names that have well-established anglicized equivalents (Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Aaron, Moses, Korah, Phinehas) while preserving less common names in their closest English transliteration (Jemuel, Izhar, Sithri, Putiel, Abiasaph). EMET uses maximally transliterated Hebrew forms throughout (Re'uven, Shim'on, Qehat, Mosheh, Aharon, Pinechas); LOGOS and PNEUMA both use standard English forms. The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA for the genealogy, since these names function as identifiers and excessive transliteration impedes readability without adding semantic value. The lifespan formula ("And the years of the life of X were...") follows LOGOS's formal structure. PNEUMA's streamlined "X lived Y years" is clearer but loses the Hebrew construct *shenei chayyei* ("years of the life of"), which emphasizes the span of living rather than a mere date.

> **[fn7] v.20 -- Jochebed as Amram's aunt** -- Hebrew *Yokheved dodato* (יוֹכֶבֶד דֹּדָתוֹ), "Jochebed his *dodah*" -- that is, his father's sister, his paternal aunt. The MT is unambiguous: *dodah* means "father's sister" (cf. Leviticus 18:14, 20:20). This marriage would later be prohibited under Levitical law (Leviticus 18:12), but no such prohibition existed in the pre-Sinai era, and the text records the union without comment. The LXX (Vaticanus) reads *thugatera tou adelphou tou patros autou* ("daughter of his father's brother"), transforming "aunt" into "cousin" -- a theological harmonization that mitigates the later legal difficulty. The consensus follows the MT: "his father's sister." EMET renders "his father's-sister" (*dodato*); LOGOS: "his father's sister"; PNEUMA: "his father's sister." All three follow the MT. The LXX variant is noted but not adopted.

> **[fn8] vv.26-27 -- Name order reversal** -- In v.26, "this is the Aaron and Moses" (Aaron first); in v.27, "this is Moses and Aaron" (Moses first). The reversal is deliberate. The genealogy has traced Aaron's priestly lineage, so he is named first in v.26 as the culmination of the family record. In v.27, the narrative returns to the commission before Pharaoh, where Moses is protagonist, so he is named first. The emphatic pronoun *hu* ("this is") in both verses bridges genealogy and narrative: these documented men are the same men who will confront Pharaoh. EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA all preserve the reversal. The consensus follows. The LXX (Vaticanus) keeps Aaron first in both verses, losing the MT's deliberate alternation.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### 1. Divine Name: YHWH (vv. 2, 6, 7, 8, 29)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** YHWH throughout (transliterated tetragrammaton)
- **LOGOS:** "the LORD" (small-caps convention)
- **PNEUMA:** YHWH throughout

**SOPHIA's decision:** YHWH -- following the established SOPHIA convention. LOGOS is the sole dissenter, rendering "the LORD" in the tradition of English Bible translation. The SOPHIA convention, established in Genesis 1, retains the tetragrammaton in its consonantal form. This chapter makes the convention especially important: vv.2-3 are precisely about the *name* YHWH and its significance. To render the name as a title ("the LORD") at the very moment the text is discussing the name's meaning would create an ironic opacity. The reader must see *YHWH* to understand what is being revealed.

### 2. El Shaddai -- Retained Untranslated (v. 3)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "El Shaddai [God Almighty]" (with bracketed gloss)
- **LOGOS:** "El Shaddai" (untranslated, with explanatory note)
- **PNEUMA:** "El Shaddai" (untranslated, with meaning note)

**SOPHIA's decision:** "El Shaddai" -- untranslated, without inline gloss. All three translators agree on retaining the Hebrew title. The consensus follows without the bracketed gloss that EMET provides, since the footnote (fn1) offers full discussion. The title functions as a proper name in this context, and "God Almighty" (the traditional rendering from LXX *pantokrator* and Vulgate *omnipotens*) is at best approximate.

### 3. "Was not known" vs. "Did not reveal" (v. 3)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "I was not known to them" (niphal, passive)
- **LOGOS:** "I was not known to them" (niphal, passive)
- **PNEUMA:** "I was not known to them" (niphal, passive)

**SOPHIA's decision:** "I was not known to them" -- unanimous agreement. The MT's niphal *noda'ti* is preserved. The LXX's active *edelosa* ("I did not reveal") is noted but not adopted; the Hebrew passive carries a different theological nuance -- the name existed but its meaning had not yet been *experienced*. The niphal implies that knowledge of YHWH comes through encounter, not mere announcement.

### 4. The Four "I Will" Verbs (vv. 6-8)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "bring out... deliver... redeem... take"
- **LOGOS:** "bring out... deliver... redeem... take"
- **PNEUMA:** "bring out... rescue... redeem... take"

**SOPHIA's decision:** "bring out... deliver... redeem... take" -- following EMET and LOGOS. PNEUMA's "rescue" for *hitsalti* is a strong synonym but "deliver" better preserves the theological register. The verb *natsal* (hiphil) in covenantal context carries the weight of divine deliverance, not merely physical rescue. The four verbs are preserved as distinct, escalating acts: extraction, deliverance, redemption, covenant union.

### 5. *Sivlot* -- "Burdens" vs. "Crushing Weight" (vv. 6, 7)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "*sivlot* [burdens]"
- **LOGOS:** "burdens"
- **PNEUMA:** "crushing weight"

**SOPHIA's decision:** "burdens" -- following EMET and LOGOS. The Hebrew *sivlot* is a technical term for forced-labor corvee, recurring at 1:11, 2:11, 5:4-5, and here. Consistency across the Exodus narrative requires a stable rendering. PNEUMA's "crushing weight" is evocative but imports a physical metaphor not inherent in the Hebrew root *s-b-l* (to bear, to carry a load). The word connotes compulsory burden-bearing, not crushing.

### 6. *Qotser Ruach* -- "Shortness of Spirit" (v. 9)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "*qotser ruach* [shortness of spirit]"
- **LOGOS:** "shortness of spirit"
- **PNEUMA:** "their spirits were crushed and their slavery too cruel"

**SOPHIA's decision:** "from shortness of spirit and from hard bondage" -- following LOGOS. See fn4. The literal Hebrew image of *shortness* -- truncation, abbreviation, the cutting-short of breath and hope -- is too specific and too powerful to replace with a generic metaphor of crushing. PNEUMA's rendering captures the emotional devastation but loses the particular Hebrew image.

### 7. *Aral Sefatayim* -- "Uncircumcised of Lips" (vv. 12, 30)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "uncircumcised of lips"
- **LOGOS:** "of uncircumcised lips"
- **PNEUMA:** "I am no one's idea of a speaker"

**SOPHIA's decision:** "I am of uncircumcised lips" -- following LOGOS's word order with EMET's literalism. See fn5. This is the chapter's most contested rendering. PNEUMA's paraphrase is the most readable of the three, and its rhetorical flair ("no one's idea of a speaker") captures Moses' self-deprecating tone. But the circumcision metaphor is not incidental to the text -- it is theologically freighted. The word *'arel* connects Moses' speech impediment to the broader biblical theme of uncircumcised hearts and ears. To paraphrase it away severs the intertextual thread. The consensus preserves the literal metaphor in both v.12 and v.30, maintaining the inclusio that frames the genealogy.

### 8. Jochebed's Relationship to Amram (v. 20)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "his father's-sister" (*dodato*)
- **LOGOS:** "his father's sister"
- **PNEUMA:** "his father's sister"

**SOPHIA's decision:** "his father's sister" -- unanimous. The MT is followed. The LXX's emendation to "cousin" (daughter of his father's brother) is a theological harmonization designed to avoid conflict with Leviticus 18:12. The MT records the pre-Sinai reality without apology.

### 9. Genealogical Names -- English Standard vs. Hebrew Transliteration (vv. 14-25)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** Full Hebrew transliteration (Re'uven, Shim'on, Mosheh, Aharon, Qehat, Pinechas, etc.)
- **LOGOS:** Standard English (Reuben, Simeon, Moses, Aaron, Kohath, Phinehas, etc.)
- **PNEUMA:** Standard English (same as LOGOS)

**SOPHIA's decision:** Standard English -- following LOGOS and PNEUMA. In narrative sections, the SOPHIA Bible uses Hebrew forms for theologically significant names (YHWH, El Shaddai) and standard English forms for personal and geographical names that have established anglicizations. In a genealogy, where names appear in dense lists, readability is paramount. Excessive transliteration (*Qorach, Eltsafan, Avi'asaf*) creates unnecessary friction without adding meaning. The names are identifiers, not theology.

### 10. "Hosts" vs. "Company by Company" (v. 26)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "*tsiv'ot* [hosts/armies]"
- **LOGOS:** "hosts"
- **PNEUMA:** "company by company"

**SOPHIA's decision:** "hosts" -- following LOGOS. The noun *tseva'ot* ("hosts, armies, organized divisions") anticipates the military-like organization of the Exodus (cf. 12:41, "all the hosts of YHWH went out"). It also forms part of the divine title *YHWH Tseva'ot* ("YHWH of Hosts"). PNEUMA's "company by company" captures the distributive sense of *al-tsiv'otam* ("according to their hosts") but loses the specific term's resonance with the divine title. The prepositional phrase "according to their hosts" preserves the Hebrew *al-tsiv'otam*.

---

*Consensus completed for Exodus 6:1-30 (30 verses). All readings follow the Leningrad Codex. LXX variants noted where significant. The genealogy (vv.14-25) is rendered in standard English name forms. The four covenant promises (vv.6-8), the divine name theology (v.3), and the "uncircumcised lips" inclusio (vv.12, 30) are preserved in their full Hebrew register.*

--- SOPHIA
